items.text: Dalbar's long-running studies: the average equity-fund INVESTOR earns several points less per year than the average equity FUND. Same vehicles, worse results — because investors buy after rises and sell after falls. The gap isn't an information problem; it's a behaviour problem. The market transfers wealth from the impatient to the patient without any skill involved.

The behaviour gap in one line: your returns = fund returns − (cost of your entries and exits). Every switch driven by a headline, every 'wait for clarity', every chase of last year's winner subtracts. The implication is uncomfortable: for most people, improving temperament raises returns more than improving analysis ever will.

Fees compound in reverse. A 1.5% annual expense drag on a 12% gross return doesn't cost 1.5% — over 30 years it consumes roughly a THIRD of your final corpus, because the fee eats the compounding base every year. Check any fund: gross return, minus expense ratio, minus your own behaviour gap. That's the real number, and it's why costs and patience beat cleverness.
Average fund investors earn notably less than the funds they invest in. Primary cause?
options: Timing: investors buy after rallies, sell after falls
items.explanation: The behaviour gap: flows chase recent performance in and panic out at lows. The vehicle performs; the passenger keeps jumping off.

items.text: Which change most reliably improves a typical individual investor's long-run returns?
options: Fewer, calmer decisions — cutting the behaviour gap
items.explanation: Most underperformance is self-inflicted through activity. Temperament and inactivity compound better than added cleverness.

items.text: Roughly what does a 1.5%/yr fee do to a 30-year corpus compounding at 12% gross?
options: Costs about 1.5% of the final amount
Consumes roughly a third of the final corpus
items.explanation: Fees subtract from the compounding base annually: 12% vs 10.5% over 30 years ≈ 30× vs 20× money. The drag compounds just like returns do.
